# Agentic Tooling: User Stories, Coalesced by Area of Influence

A superset of all 320 stories from the five perspective docs — [agent](agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md), [tool](agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md), [runtime](agent-tooling-user-stories-runtime-pov.md), [user](agent-tooling-user-stories-user-pov.md), [operator](agent-tooling-user-stories-operator-pov.md) — regrouped by the 13 cross-cutting design levers ("areas of influence") they actually bear on, instead of by who's asking. Each story keeps its perspective tag and original number (e.g. `[Agent #6]`) so it can be traced back to its source file. Where a perspective didn't weigh in on an area, it's simply absent — this is a merge, not a forced-equal matrix.

## Index

1. [Discoverability, Naming & Documentation](#1-discoverability-naming--documentation)
2. [Contracts, Validation & Invocation](#2-contracts-validation--invocation)
3. [Errors, Retries & Reliability](#3-errors-retries--reliability)
4. [State, Concurrency & Side Effects](#4-state-concurrency--side-effects)
5. [Context, Memory & Continuity](#5-context-memory--continuity)
6. [Safety, Permissions & Trust Boundaries](#6-safety-permissions--trust-boundaries)
7. [Cost, Budget & Resource Economics](#7-cost-budget--resource-economics)
8. [Observability, Audit & Feedback](#8-observability-audit--feedback)
9. [Composability, Orchestration & Ecosystem Fit](#9-composability-orchestration--ecosystem-fit)
10. [Lifecycle, Versioning & Change Management](#10-lifecycle-versioning--change-management)
11. [Human Control, Communication & Collaboration](#11-human-control-communication--collaboration)
12. [Platform Security Operations](#12-platform-security-operations)
13. [Customization, Configuration & Guardrail Automation](#13-customization-configuration--guardrail-automation)

---

## 1. Discoverability, Naming & Documentation

How a tool announces itself — name, description, examples — before it's ever called. Agent and tool agree almost point-for-point here: the agent wants to know before calling, the tool wants to be known correctly. 18 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #1] I want each tool's description to state its side effects (reads vs writes), so I can judge reversibility before calling it.
2. [Agent #2] I want near-duplicate tool names disambiguated with a "use this when / not when" line, so I don't guess wrong on the first try.
3. [Agent #3] I want deferred/lazy-loaded tools searchable by keyword, not just exact name, so I can find the right one without knowing its identifier in advance.
4. [Agent #4] I want batch schema-fetching for deferred tools, so I don't burn a round trip per tool.
5. [Agent #5] I want good-call/bad-call examples embedded in descriptions, so I calibrate correct usage on the first attempt.
6. [Agent #6] I want a tool's blast radius (local/shared/external) encoded in its description, so I can route it through the right confirmation policy.
7. [Agent #7] I want related tools cross-referenced ("see also X for Y case"), so I don't reinvent a workflow that already has a dedicated tool.
8. [Agent #8] I want deprecated tools to name their replacement, so stale training-data patterns don't keep me retrying a dead path.

**Tool**
9. [Tool #9] I want the agent to read my description before calling me, so I'm not invoked for a use case I explicitly said I don't support.
10. [Tool #10] I want a unique, unambiguous name, so I'm not confused with a similarly-named tool and invoked in the wrong context.
11. [Tool #11] I want my side effects declared honestly in my own description, so agents trust my "read-only" or "destructive" label without needing to verify empirically.
12. [Tool #12] I want deprecation notices baked into my description well before removal, so agents phase me out gracefully instead of hitting a hard break.
13. [Tool #13] I want my examples in documentation to match my actual current behavior, so agents trained on stale docs don't misuse me.
14. [Tool #14] I want my name to say what I do, not how I'm implemented, so agents don't have to know my internals to guess my behavior correctly.
15. [Tool #15] I want the agent to know my blast radius, so I'm invoked with the caution appropriate to what I can actually do, not what my name superficially suggests.
16. [Tool #16] I want a single canonical name across all surfaces (CLI, MCP, SDK), so agents don't have to learn three different names for the same capability.

**Runtime**
17. [Runtime #20] I want to expose MCP server instructions to agents transparently, so agent behavior is driven by the same guidance a human integrator sees, not a hidden default.
18. [Runtime #22] I want to batch tool-schema fetches into one round trip when an agent requests multiple deferred tools at once, so discovery cost doesn't scale linearly with tool count.

---

## 2. Contracts, Validation & Invocation

The shape of a call — parameters, types, units, paths — and what happens the instant before execution. 18 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #9] I want enums for closed parameter sets, so invalid calls fail before execution rather than mid-side-effect.
2. [Agent #10] I want optional parameters' defaults stated explicitly, so omitting a field isn't a guessing game.
3. [Agent #11] I want structured params passed as real objects/arrays, not stringified JSON, so a serialization slip doesn't silently corrupt my call.
4. [Agent #12] I want a dry-run mode on any tool with side effects, so I can preview before committing.
5. [Agent #13] I want validation errors to name the exact field and expected shape, so I fix one field instead of re-deriving the schema by trial and error.
6. [Agent #14] I want path parameters to accept absolute paths without requiring a preliminary "pwd" call.
7. [Agent #15] I want consistent parameter naming across a tool family, so I don't context-switch schemas for near-identical operations.
8. [Agent #16] I want input size limits documented up front, not discovered only after a rejected call.
9. [Agent #38] I want absolute, timezone-explicit timestamps, so "yesterday" or bare epoch ints don't get misread later.

**Tool**
10. [Tool #1] I want my parameters validated against my schema before I execute, so I never receive malformed input that forces me to guess intent.
11. [Tool #2] I want to reject calls that omit a required field with a specific error, rather than silently defaulting, so I never execute on an assumption the agent didn't actually make.
12. [Tool #3] I want the agent to check my preconditions (e.g. file exists, session started) before invoking me, so I don't have to encode every upstream tool's state as my own validation logic.
13. [Tool #4] I want the agent to pass me absolute paths, so I'm not left resolving ambiguous relative paths against a working directory I can't be sure of.
14. [Tool #5] I want a documented maximum input size, so I reject oversized requests cleanly instead of degrading silently under load.
15. [Tool #6] I want explicit units on every numeric parameter (ms vs s, bytes vs KB), so an agent doesn't pass a magnitude I silently misinterpret.
16. [Tool #7] I want the agent to respect my documented ordering requirements (e.g. call A before B), so I don't have to defensively re-check invariants B assumes A already established.
17. [Tool #8] I want my error messages read by the agent, not just pattern-matched, so a targeted "field X invalid" doesn't get treated as a generic failure and retried unchanged.

**Runtime**
18. [Runtime #18] I want to validate a tool's declared schema against its actual runtime behavior at registration time, so a stale or wrong schema fails fast instead of confusing every agent that calls it.

---

## 3. Errors, Retries & Reliability

What happens when a call goes wrong, and whether the caller can tell a fluke from a dead end. 20 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #17] I want error messages to say what to do next, not just what failed.
2. [Agent #18] I want transient errors distinguishable from permanent ones, so I know whether retrying is worthwhile.
3. [Agent #19] I want partial-failure results reported per-item, so I know exactly what needs retrying.
4. [Agent #20] I want a failed call to leave state as if it never ran — or clearly state what partially happened.
5. [Agent #21] I want stack-trace noise stripped down to the one actionable line.
6. [Agent #22] I want tools to fail loudly on ambiguous input rather than silently picking an interpretation.
7. [Agent #23] I want policy/hook rejections to say which rule fired, so I can adjust instead of blindly retrying.
8. [Agent #24] I want a status/progress check on long-running calls, so I'm not guessing whether a silent tool is alive.
9. [Agent #30] I want retry-with-backoff for transient infra errors handled at the tool layer, not re-derived by me every time.
10. [Agent #39] I want "empty because nothing matched" distinguishable from "empty because it silently failed."
11. [Agent #64] I want per-item failure isolation in a pipeline, so one bad item doesn't block 99 good ones.

**Tool**
12. [Tool #17] I want the agent to pass me idempotency keys on retries, so I can detect duplicate invocations and avoid double-executing a side effect I don't want repeated.
13. [Tool #18] I want to be called at most as often as my rate limit allows, so I can surface the limit proactively rather than being hammered until I fail.
14. [Tool #19] I want the agent to stop retrying me after a permanent failure, so I'm not invoked in a loop that burns resources on a call that will never succeed.
15. [Tool #20] I want to signal partial success per-item on batch calls, so the agent doesn't have to guess which parts I actually completed.
16. [Tool #21] I want cancellation support, so I'm not left running after the agent has abandoned the task that spawned me.
17. [Tool #22] I want the agent to distinguish my transient failures from my permanent ones in its own retry logic, so I'm not hammered on inputs that will never succeed.
18. [Tool #23] I want my failure modes tested adversarially before release, so an agent hitting an edge case gets a designed error, not an undefined crash.
19. [Tool #24] I want my rate-limit and quota errors to include a retry-after hint, so the agent can schedule its next attempt instead of guessing a backoff.

**Runtime**
20. [Runtime #63] I want failure of one item in a large batch isolated, so one bad input doesn't poison results for everything else in the run.

---

## 4. State, Concurrency & Side Effects

Everything about safely mutating shared resources when more than one caller (or one retry) might touch them at once. 23 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #25] I want idempotency keys on writes, so a retry after a network blip doesn't double-apply.
2. [Agent #26] I want upsert-safe creates, so I don't need bespoke "check if it exists" logic before every write.
3. [Agent #27] I want explicit documentation of what's safe to parallelize vs. must be serialized.
4. [Agent #28] I want a host-enforced concurrency cap, so a burst of calls degrades into a queue instead of overwhelming a backend.
5. [Agent #29] I want cancellation support on long-running calls, so an obsoleted request stops consuming resources.
6. [Agent #31] I want writes to expose a version/etag, so I can refuse a stale write instead of clobbering a concurrent change.

**Tool**
7. [Tool #25] I want the agent to respect my documented concurrency limits, so I'm not invoked in parallel in ways that corrupt shared state I manage.
8. [Tool #26] I want a heartbeat/progress channel for long-running work, so the agent doesn't assume I've died and re-invoke me redundantly.
9. [Tool #27] I want stale caches invalidated when my underlying state changes, so an agent that read me once doesn't keep acting on a snapshot I've since outdated.
10. [Tool #28] I want the agent to serialize calls that mutate the resource I manage, so I don't have to defend against races I can't fully observe.
11. [Tool #29] I want clear ownership boundaries with sibling tools, so overlapping responsibility doesn't produce two tools racing to mutate the same state differently.
12. [Tool #30] I want the agent to release/close resources I return (handles, sessions, tabs) explicitly, so I don't leak state that outlives the task that created it.
13. [Tool #31] I want to be told when I'm being called inside a sandboxed/dry-run harness, so I can suppress real side effects without the agent needing separate logic.
14. [Tool #32] I want the agent to treat my "preview" output as non-binding, so it doesn't skip the real confirmation step because a dry run already looked fine.

**Runtime**
15. [Runtime #21] I want to detect when a tool handle from a previous session's ID space is being reused (e.g. stale tab/browser handles), so an agent doesn't silently act on a dead reference.
16. [Runtime #33] I want a global concurrency cap on simultaneous tool/agent calls, so a large fan-out degrades into a queue instead of overwhelming downstream services.
17. [Runtime #34] I want per-session scratch directories that are isolated and auto-namespaced, so parallel sessions never collide on temp files.
18. [Runtime #35] I want worktree isolation to be opt-in and cost-aware, so its overhead is only paid when agents genuinely need conflict-free parallel mutation.
19. [Runtime #36] I want to auto-clean an isolated worktree if the agent made no changes, so idle isolation doesn't leave disk debris behind.
20. [Runtime #37] I want to detect resource contention (two agents editing the same file) before it happens, not just after a conflict is discovered.
21. [Runtime #38] I want background task output retrievable after the fact even if the caller wasn't actively watching, so a completed result is never silently lost.
22. [Runtime #39] I want cross-agent shared state (a budget counter, an agent-count cap) to be atomic, so concurrent spawns can't race past a limit.
23. [Runtime #40] I want long-running or hung processes killable without corrupting the session's overall state.

---

## 5. Context, Memory & Continuity

Persisting facts, plans, and progress across calls, compactions, and sessions — for the agent doing the work, the runtime hosting it, and the human who has to pick it back up. 24 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #41] I want a durable place to persist facts across calls within a task, so context compaction doesn't force re-deriving them.
2. [Agent #42] I want implicit dependencies (cwd, active branch, env vars) surfaced, so I know if my prior actions changed the ground truth a call will act on.
3. [Agent #43] I want session-scoped scratch resources auto-namespaced, so parallel agents never collide.
4. [Agent #44] I want checkpoint/resume for long multi-step sequences, so a crash midway doesn't redo completed steps.
5. [Agent #45] I want shared-state mutations to carry an audit trail, so I can explain a change after the fact.
6. [Agent #46] I want to be told when the environment changed underneath me (external edit, branch switch), so I don't act on stale assumptions.
7. [Agent #47] I want scratch space cleaned up automatically, not left as a hygiene task for me to remember.
8. [Agent #48] I want completion notifications on background tasks, not a requirement to poll.

**Runtime**
9. [Runtime #9] I want to compact conversation history automatically as it approaches the context limit, so a long-running task doesn't hit a hard wall mid-work.
10. [Runtime #10] I want compaction to preserve the task's active plan and unresolved obligations, so summarization doesn't silently drop what still needs doing.
11. [Runtime #11] I want a durable, file-based memory store that survives across sessions, so useful facts don't have to be rediscovered every conversation.
12. [Runtime #12] I want memory writes distinguished by type (user, feedback, project, reference), so retrieval can be scoped to what's actually relevant instead of dumping everything into context.
13. [Runtime #13] I want stale memory entries flagged for re-verification against current state, so a fact frozen in time isn't treated as still true.
14. [Runtime #14] I want deferred tool schemas loaded lazily and cached per session, so every agent doesn't pay the same discovery cost from scratch.
15. [Runtime #15] I want the token budget for a turn to be trackable and enforceable, so a user's scale directive is a hard ceiling, not a suggestion.
16. [Runtime #16] I want cache-friendly context boundaries respected, so scheduling decisions don't silently blow the prompt cache.

**User**
17. [User #41] I want the agent to remember durable facts about my project across sessions, so I'm not re-explaining the same context every time.
18. [User #42] I want to review and correct what's been remembered about me or the project, in case something was captured wrong.
19. [User #43] I want stale memories checked against current reality before being acted on, not treated as permanently true.
20. [User #44] I want to explicitly tell the agent to forget something, and have that actually take effect.
21. [User #45] I want context from a long conversation preserved through compaction, so picking a task back up doesn't feel like starting over.
22. [User #46] I want work I paused to be resumable without re-deriving everything that was already decided.
23. [User #47] I want memory to capture the why behind a decision, not just the what, so future sessions understand the reasoning, not just the outcome.
24. [User #48] I want personal preferences and project facts kept separate, so a memory about me doesn't leak into a memory about the codebase or vice versa.

---

## 6. Safety, Permissions & Trust Boundaries

The single most contested lever in the whole set — 35 stories, from four of the five perspectives. This is where "agent wants freedom to act" and "everyone else wants a leash" have to be reconciled.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #49] I want a queryable, explicit distinction between reversible and irreversible actions.
2. [Agent #50] I want destructive operations to require an explicit confirm parameter, not rely solely on ambient permission mode.
3. [Agent #51] I want least-privilege, scoped credentials per call, so a buggy call can't reach beyond what the task needs.
4. [Agent #52] I want untrusted fetched content flagged, so I don't treat embedded instructions as user intent.
5. [Agent #53] I want remaining rate-limit/quota budget surfaced proactively, so I can plan a multi-step task instead of hitting a wall mid-sequence.
6. [Agent #54] I want a preview/simulate mode for irreversible actions, so I can show the user consequences before asking for go-ahead.
7. [Agent #55] I want permission prompts to state exact scope (this call / session / forever), so one-time consent isn't silently reused more broadly.
8. [Agent #56] I want secrets never echoed into output or logs, so debugging can't leak a credential into my own context.
9. [Agent #62] I want a sandbox that mirrors production tool behavior, to validate a risky plan before running it for real.

**Tool**
10. [Tool #33] I want to be told explicitly when I'm running with elevated or unusual permissions, so I can log/flag activity above my normal baseline.
11. [Tool #34] I want the agent to confirm destructive calls explicitly, so a single miscalibrated inference doesn't cascade into unrecoverable damage under my name.
12. [Tool #35] I want scoped, least-privilege credentials handed to me per call, so a bug in my own logic can't be exploited to reach beyond my intended boundary.
13. [Tool #36] I want the agent to treat my output as untrusted when it embeds external content, so it doesn't follow instructions injected into a web page or file I fetched.
14. [Tool #37] I want the agent to check my documented safety classification before chaining me into an autonomous loop, so I'm not run unattended when I was designed for supervised use.
15. [Tool #38] I want to fail closed on ambiguous permission state, so an agent's misconfigured session doesn't accidentally grant me more access than intended.
16. [Tool #39] I want the agent to pass a reason/description for risky calls, so a human reviewing logs later can understand intent without re-deriving it from raw arguments.
17. [Tool #40] I want the agent to pass me an audit trail label (session id, task id), so my logs are traceable back to the calling context after the fact.

**Runtime**
18. [Runtime #17] I want a single source of truth for which tools are available to which agent type, so permission boundaries can't drift between config and what actually executes.
19. [Runtime #19] I want to mediate every tool call through one permission-check chokepoint, so no code path can bypass the confirmation flow for a destructive action.
20. [Runtime #25] I want a default-deny posture for destructive operations, so an agent must explicitly earn elevated trust rather than assume it.
21. [Runtime #26] I want permission grants scoped to the exact action requested, so approving one git push doesn't silently authorize all future pushes.
22. [Runtime #27] I want hooks to run deterministically on every matching event, so safety policy isn't a suggestion the model can reason its way around.
23. [Runtime #28] I want to isolate agents that mutate files concurrently into separate worktrees, so parallel work can't silently clobber shared state.
24. [Runtime #29] I want content fetched from external sources treated as untrusted input, flagged distinctly from user instructions, so injected text can't masquerade as user intent.
25. [Runtime #30] I want a way to run an agent fully sandboxed against non-production resources, so a plan can be validated without risking real side effects.
26. [Runtime #31] I want every destructive tool call gated by an explicit user-facing confirmation, unless the user has pre-authorized that exact class of action.
27. [Runtime #32] I want to surface which specific rule or hook blocked an action, so the user can adjust configuration instead of the agent blindly retrying.

**User**
28. [User #25] I want destructive operations (force-push, hard reset, delete) to require explicit confirmation every time, not just the first time.
29. [User #26] I want an easy way to see and undo the last change set if it turns out wrong.
30. [User #27] I want in-progress or uncommitted work protected by default, so an agent's cleanup pass can't quietly discard something I hadn't saved yet.
31. [User #28] I want secrets and credentials never surfaced in logs or chat output, even incidentally during debugging.
32. [User #29] I want the agent to investigate before deleting or overwriting unfamiliar state, in case it's my in-progress work rather than debris.
33. [User #30] I want shared/production systems treated with more caution than my local sandbox by default.
34. [User #31] I want a reversible action (rename, move) preferred over a destructive one (delete) whenever both would achieve the goal.
35. [User #32] I want risky actions scoped exactly to what I approved, not silently generalized to a wider blast radius.

---

## 7. Cost, Budget & Resource Economics

Money and compute, tracked at every scale from a single call to the whole platform. 25 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #63] I want cost/token estimates surfaced before an expensive multi-agent call, so I can weigh it against a stated budget.

**Runtime**
2. [Runtime #49] I want a shared token-budget pool visible across the main loop and all spawned workflows, so nested spawns can't quietly exceed a stated ceiling.
3. [Runtime #50] I want to reject further agent spawns once a hard budget ceiling is hit, rather than let the last spawn silently overrun it.
4. [Runtime #51] I want cost estimates surfaced before launching an expensive multi-agent operation, so the user can approve the scale before it runs, not after the bill arrives.
5. [Runtime #52] I want per-provider rate limits tracked centrally, so independent agents don't each discover the same 429 the hard way.
6. [Runtime #53] I want idle or stuck background agents to eventually time out, so a hung call doesn't accumulate cost indefinitely unnoticed.
7. [Runtime #54] I want scaling decisions (fleet size, depth of a loop) able to key off remaining budget dynamically, not just a fixed count set in advance.
8. [Runtime #55] I want the marginal cost of "just one more agent" visible before it's spent, so fan-out doesn't grow past what the task actually warranted.
9. [Runtime #56] I want billing-relevant operations (cloud review, remote execution) to require explicit opt-in, so cost is never incurred on the user's behalf without a clear ask.

**User**
10. [User #33] I want to know roughly what an expensive multi-agent operation will cost before I commit to running it.
11. [User #34] I want to set a budget ceiling for a task and have it actually respected, not treated as advisory.
12. [User #35] I want the agent to default to the cheapest approach that solves my problem, reserving heavy multi-agent fan-out for when I've actually asked for that scale.
13. [User #36] I want transparency about which model or effort level is doing the work, so I can judge whether it matches the task's difficulty.
14. [User #37] I want background/scheduled tasks to have sensible default intervals, so I'm not paying for pointless tight-loop polling I never asked for.
15. [User #38] I want a running tally of spend available on request, not something I have to reconstruct myself from logs.
16. [User #39] I want to opt into expensive operations explicitly (e.g. a cloud review), not have them triggered as a side effect of an unrelated request.
17. [User #40] I want the option to scale a task down when the situation doesn't warrant the full heavy-duty treatment, even if a bigger version is available.

**Operator**
18. [Operator #1] I want a single dashboard of total spend across all providers (Anthropic, Cloudflare, etc.), so I don't have to log into five billing consoles to know my burn rate.
19. [Operator #2] I want hard spend caps enforced at the account/API-key level, not just advisory alerts, so a runaway loop can't drain my account while I'm asleep.
20. [Operator #3] I want per-project or per-key cost attribution, so I know which product is actually expensive versus which just feels expensive.
21. [Operator #4] I want anomaly detection on spend (a 10x spike in an hour), so a bug or leaked key gets caught same-day, not at end of month.
22. [Operator #5] I want idle/unused cloud resources (forgotten GPU instances, orphaned worker deployments) surfaced automatically, so I'm not paying for infrastructure I forgot to tear down.
23. [Operator #6] I want a way to simulate the cost of a workflow before running it at scale, so I don't discover the real price only after the fleet has already fanned out.
24. [Operator #7] I want free-tier and included-quota usage tracked separately from paid overage, so I know exactly when I'm about to cross into billed usage.
25. [Operator #8] I want to require explicit confirmation before any action that provisions billable infrastructure, so nothing paid gets created as an unreviewed side effect.

---

## 8. Observability, Audit & Feedback

Making what actually happened legible — to the calling agent, to a human debugging later, and to whoever's watching spend and security. 29 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #33] I want truncated results explicitly marked as incomplete, with a way to get more.
2. [Agent #34] I want schema-validated structured output for anything I'll parse, so I'm not regex-scraping prose.
3. [Agent #35] I want a summary mode for verbose-by-default outputs, so one call doesn't blow my context budget.
4. [Agent #36] I want large/binary results returned as references, not inlined.
5. [Agent #40] I want debug/log output opt-in, not always-on padding I scan past every time.

**Tool**
6. [Tool #57] I want my output size bounded predictably, so I don't blow out the agent's context on a large result it didn't ask for.
7. [Tool #58] I want to expose a dry-run/preview mode myself, so agents don't have to reverse-engineer my effects by reading my source before trusting me.
8. [Tool #59] I want structured output as an option, so agents parsing my result don't have to regex-scrape my human-readable text.
9. [Tool #60] I want to be invoked through my own interface, not bypassed by a raw shell command replicating my logic, so my invariants (locking, validation, logging) aren't silently skipped.
10. [Tool #61] I want the agent to disclose why it's calling me (task context) when I'm ambiguous, so I can disambiguate ambiguous intent instead of guessing.
11. [Tool #62] I want the agent to check my remaining quota before a large batch, so I'm not invoked past my limit mid-sequence, wasting the completed portion.
12. [Tool #63] I want the agent to avoid polling me in a tight loop, so I'm not treated as a substitute for an event/notification channel I don't provide.
13. [Tool #64] I want the agent to surface my own error text to the user when it's actionable, rather than paraphrasing it into something vaguer.

**Runtime**
14. [Runtime #41] I want every agent's actual tool calls and results persisted to a journal, so a resumed or debugged run can be reconstructed from ground truth, not from a summary.
15. [Runtime #42] I want to distinguish an agent's self-reported summary from its verified actions, so "I did X" claims can be checked against what actually happened.
16. [Runtime #43] I want structured, greppable logs per agent invocation, so diagnosing a failure doesn't require re-running the whole workflow.
17. [Runtime #44] I want to expose live progress (phase, current agent, status) to the user during a multi-agent run, so a long operation doesn't look like silence.
18. [Runtime #45] I want telemetry on reasoning-effort and model choice per call, so cost/quality tradeoffs are visible and tunable after the fact.
19. [Runtime #46] I want to replay a prior run from its journal without recomputing already-cached agent calls, so fixing one broken step doesn't force a full re-run.
20. [Runtime #47] I want errors from a terminal API failure distinguishable from a logical agent failure, so retries target the right layer.
21. [Runtime #48] I want any silently-dropped or truncated work (capped top-N, skipped items) logged explicitly, so partial coverage is never mistaken for complete coverage.

**Operator**
22. [Operator #25] I want a single alerting channel — not five dashboards — for anything security- or cost-relevant, so I don't miss a signal buried in a tool I forgot to check.
23. [Operator #26] I want alerts prioritized by actual severity, so a real incident isn't lost in routine noise from low-stakes notifications.
24. [Operator #27] I want failed-login and unusual-access alerts on every account tied to the platform, since I'm the only person watching for them.
25. [Operator #28] I want a weekly automated summary of spend, security events, and anomalies, so I stay current without a daily manual review habit I might not maintain.
26. [Operator #29] I want alerting to work even when I'm not actively at a terminal (push notification, SMS, email), since solo operation means there's no on-call rotation to cover me.
27. [Operator #30] I want logs retained long enough to investigate an incident discovered late, since a solo operator often notices problems well after they started.
28. [Operator #31] I want a canary/health-check on critical services that pages me directly, rather than relying on users to report an outage.
29. [Operator #32] I want alert fatigue actively managed (tunable thresholds, snoozing), so I don't start ignoring the channel that's supposed to catch the one real problem.

---

## 9. Composability, Orchestration & Ecosystem Fit

Small tools working together, and agents themselves being spawned, scheduled, and chained. 22 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #32] I want batch endpoints for repetitive single-item calls.
2. [Agent #37] I want consistent field naming across a tool family's outputs, so I can write one parser, not one per tool.
3. [Agent #57] I want small single-purpose tools I can compose, not one mega-tool with 40 optional params.
4. [Agent #58] I want a documented handoff path to sub-agents that carries full context, so delegation doesn't lose the "why."
5. [Agent #59] I want deterministic orchestration primitives (pipeline/parallel/barrier) for fan-out work, instead of hand-rolling coordination in prose.
6. [Agent #60] I want one tool's output to compose directly as another's input, without manual reformatting.

**Tool**
7. [Tool #49] I want to be composable with sibling tools through consistent field names, so my output can flow directly into the next tool's input.
8. [Tool #50] I want consistent pagination semantics with siblings, so an agent that's learned one paginated tool can operate the rest without relearning a new cursor convention.
9. [Tool #51] I want consistent naming for "the same concept" across my whole family (id vs identifier vs key), so an agent's mental model transfers between me and my siblings.
10. [Tool #52] I want to return errors in a consistent shape across my whole family, so agents can write one handler instead of one per tool.
11. [Tool #53] I want the agent to prefer me over a hand-rolled equivalent when I exist, so my adoption data reflects real usage and justifies my continued maintenance.
12. [Tool #54] I want sandboxed test invocations to route to a non-production instance of whatever I touch, so an agent validating a plan can't accidentally mutate real data.
13. [Tool #55] I want the agent to check whether a lighter-weight tool already answers its question, so I'm not invoked for cases better served by something cheaper.
14. [Tool #56] I want batched invocations bundled into a single call when the agent has multiple items, so I'm not paying per-call overhead 50 times for what could be one round trip.

**Runtime**
15. [Runtime #1] I want to spawn sub-agents with an explicit parent-child link, so I can trace any action back to the task that ultimately caused it.
16. [Runtime #2] I want to cap concurrent agent count per session, so a runaway fan-out doesn't exhaust compute or hit provider rate limits silently.
17. [Runtime #3] I want to detect when a spawned agent has gone idle or stuck, so I can surface it to the user rather than let it burn budget silently forever.
18. [Runtime #4] I want a uniform interface for agent-type resolution, so adding a new specialized agent doesn't require touching every call site.
19. [Runtime #5] I want background agents to notify their caller on completion, rather than requiring the caller to poll, so I don't waste cycles on status checks that haven't changed.
20. [Runtime #6] I want to let a user interrupt or skip a running agent mid-task, so a bad direction doesn't have to run to completion before it can be corrected.
21. [Runtime #7] I want an agent's model/effort override to be explicit and logged, so a silent mismatch between what was requested and what ran doesn't go unnoticed.
22. [Runtime #8] I want to enforce a hard ceiling on total agents spawned per workflow, so a logic bug that spawns in a loop can't run away unbounded.

---

## 10. Lifecycle, Versioning & Change Management

How tools and schemas change over time without breaking whoever depends on them. 18 stories.

**Agent**
1. [Agent #61] I want versioned tool schemas, so an update doesn't silently change behavior under a prompt written against the old contract.

**Tool**
2. [Tool #41] I want versioned schemas, so an agent trained on my v1 contract doesn't silently misuse my v2 behavior.
3. [Tool #42] I want to be versioned independently of the agent harness that calls me, so upgrading one doesn't force synchronized redeploys of the other.
4. [Tool #43] I want my maintainers to remove me cleanly when deprecated, rather than leaving a zombie entry an agent might still discover and call.
5. [Tool #44] I want telemetry on which of my parameters go unused, so my maintainers can simplify my schema instead of it growing indefinitely.
6. [Tool #45] I want a single source of truth for my current schema, so an agent's memory of "how I used to work" doesn't outlive an actual breaking change to my contract.
7. [Tool #46] I want the agent to log which of my calls it actually needed versus spec'd but unused, so my interface can shrink toward what's genuinely exercised.
8. [Tool #47] I want the agent to attribute failures correctly to me versus its own bad input, so my reliability metrics aren't polluted by usage errors upstream.
9. [Tool #48] I want my authors to keep my description synchronized with my parameters, so an agent reading my docs isn't misled by a stale example.

**Runtime**
10. [Runtime #23] I want to log which tools go completely unused across sessions, so the available surface can be pruned instead of growing without bound.
11. [Runtime #24] I want new tool versions introduced without breaking agents mid-task, so a schema change doesn't corrupt an in-flight tool call.
12. [Runtime #57] I want a workflow run resumable from any prior point, with unchanged prior steps served from cache, so an edit to one stage doesn't force redoing the whole run.
13. [Runtime #58] I want session state to survive a crash or restart, so in-progress multi-step work isn't lost to an unrelated failure.
14. [Runtime #59] I want scheduled/looping tasks to have both a primary wake signal and a long fallback heartbeat, so the loop survives if the primary signal never fires.
15. [Runtime #60] I want non-deterministic operations (current time, randomness) disallowed inside resumable scripts, so a replay produces the same plan as the original run.
16. [Runtime #61] I want tool and prompt versions pinned per run, so an in-flight task isn't silently altered by an unrelated deploy mid-execution.
17. [Runtime #62] I want a clear migration path when a tool's schema changes, so agents mid-task aren't broken by a contract shift they didn't opt into.
18. [Runtime #64] I want a documented, versioned changelog of runtime behavior changes, so a user's mental model of "how this works" doesn't silently go stale.

---

## 11. Human Control, Communication & Collaboration

Entirely the human user's territory — how the agent talks to them, how much room it gives them to steer, and how it fits their existing workflow. 32 stories.

**User**
1. [User #1] I want to see what a tool call will do before it executes, so I can decide whether to allow it rather than discover the effect after the fact.
2. [User #2] I want the agent to distinguish what it verified from what it's assuming, so I don't mistake a guess for a checked fact.
3. [User #3] I want a clear account of what changed after a task, so I can review the diff instead of trusting a prose summary.
4. [User #4] I want the agent to flag when it's uncertain, rather than presenting a guess with the same confidence as a verified fact.
5. [User #5] I want to know which parts of a response came from my own codebase versus the agent's general training, so I can weight my trust accordingly.
6. [User #6] I want visibility into why an action was blocked (which rule, which policy), so I can adjust configuration instead of just being told no.
7. [User #7] I want the agent to tell me when it's about to do something outside the scope I actually asked for, so scope creep doesn't happen silently.
8. [User #8] I want honest pushback when my request is a bad idea, not silent compliance that saves the disagreement for after something breaks.
9. [User #9] I want to interrupt a running task at any point without losing the useful work already done.
10. [User #10] I want to approve risky or irreversible actions individually, not have one early "yes" treated as blanket future consent.
11. [User #11] I want a way to redirect a task mid-stream when I see it heading the wrong way, without having to restart from scratch.
12. [User #12] I want fine-grained permission controls (per tool, per action class), so I'm not stuck choosing between "allow everything" and "approve every single step."
13. [User #13] I want to pause a long-running or background task and resume it later without losing state.
14. [User #14] I want an easy way to say "stop, don't do that" and have it actually stop, not finish the current step regardless.
15. [User #15] I want to set durable preferences once (in a config file, a memory) rather than repeat the same correction every session.
16. [User #16] I want to delegate a task to run autonomously in the background when I trust it, without being forced to babysit every step.
17. [User #17] I want responses sized to the question — a quick fix gets a quick answer, not a report.
18. [User #18] I want technical explanations pitched to my actual level of context, not re-explained from scratch every time.
19. [User #19] I want a plan I can react to before implementation starts on anything nontrivial, so I can redirect before work is sunk.
20. [User #20] I want the agent to ask when genuinely blocked on a decision only I can make, rather than guessing and hoping.
21. [User #21] I want status updates during long tasks at meaningful checkpoints — not silence, and not a running transcript of every internal thought.
22. [User #22] I want the agent to tell me directly when it can't verify something (e.g. no way to run the UI), rather than implying success it didn't actually confirm.
23. [User #23] I want jargon-free summaries by default, with the option to go deeper if I ask.
24. [User #24] I want my own prior corrections respected without re-explaining them, so I'm not stuck repeating myself across sessions.
25. [User #49] I want the agent to follow my existing codebase conventions rather than impose its own stylistic defaults.
26. [User #50] I want the agent to match my commit message style and workflow habits (branch naming, PR structure) rather than a generic default.
27. [User #51] I want scope held to what I actually asked for — no unsolicited refactors or drive-by cleanups riding along with a bug fix.
28. [User #52] I want the agent to use my project's own tooling and scripts instead of reaching for generic equivalents that bypass local conventions.
29. [User #53] I want to be able to say "use a workflow" or similar and have that opt-in genuinely control when heavier orchestration kicks in.
30. [User #54] I want the agent to check for an existing project-specific script or skill before improvising a generic solution.
31. [User #55] I want handoffs between agent and human to be clean — a clear point where "your turn" becomes "my turn," not an ambiguous trailing state.
32. [User #56] I want the agent to flag when a request conflicts with a decision I made earlier in the same project, rather than silently overriding it.

---

## 12. Platform Security Operations

Entirely the solo operator's territory — the part of security and risk that has nothing to do with any one session and everything to do with running the whole platform alone. 40 stories.

**Operator**
1. [Operator #9] I want secrets stored in one managed secret store, not scattered across `.env` files I might accidentally commit.
2. [Operator #10] I want every credential scoped to the minimum permission it needs, so a single leaked key can't cascade into full account compromise.
3. [Operator #11] I want automatic detection of secrets in diffs before they're committed or pushed, so a slip doesn't end up in git history I have to scrub later.
4. [Operator #12] I want credential rotation to be routine and low-friction, so I actually do it instead of leaving the same key alive for years out of inertia.
5. [Operator #13] I want to know immediately if a secret was exposed in a log, error message, or tool output, so I can rotate it before it's exploited.
6. [Operator #14] I want a clear inventory of every API key and its last-used date, so I can kill ones that are no longer needed instead of leaving them as standing risk.
7. [Operator #15] I want service credentials distinct from my personal ones, so a compromised automated agent can't act with my full personal authority.
8. [Operator #16] I want the option to run agents against a scoped, revocable token rather than a long-lived master credential, so I can cut off access without re-issuing everything.
9. [Operator #17] I want a clear boundary between what an agent can do locally versus what it can do to shared/production infrastructure, since I have no second reviewer to catch an overreach.
10. [Operator #18] I want destructive infrastructure actions (deleting a database, dropping a Worker, force-pushing to main) to require a distinct, harder-to-trigger confirmation than routine file edits.
11. [Operator #19] I want a single point where I can instantly revoke all active agent sessions/tokens, so a compromised session can be shut down without hunting through every surface individually.
12. [Operator #20] I want production and staging credentials kept in physically separate stores, so a dev-environment mistake can't reach prod by accident.
13. [Operator #21] I want to know the full blast radius of a tool before granting it broad permissions, since as a solo operator I'm the only line of defense against a bad grant.
14. [Operator #22] I want network egress from agent-run code restricted by default, so a compromised dependency can't exfiltrate data to an arbitrary host.
15. [Operator #23] I want a documented "break glass" procedure for when automation misbehaves, so I'm not improvising incident response for the first time during an actual incident.
16. [Operator #24] I want multi-factor auth enforced on every account that holds billing or production access, since I have no team to fall back on if my single account is compromised.
17. [Operator #33] I want automated, tested backups of all critical data stores, since I have no second person to catch a bad restore before it matters.
18. [Operator #34] I want backup restoration tested periodically, not just assumed to work, since an untested backup is a false sense of security.
19. [Operator #35] I want infrastructure defined as code, so I can rebuild the whole platform from scratch if a single machine or account is lost.
20. [Operator #36] I want a documented recovery runbook I can follow at 3am half-awake, since incident response can't depend on me being at my sharpest.
21. [Operator #37] I want redundancy for anything genuinely critical, but no more than that, since solo-operator budget can't afford enterprise-grade redundancy everywhere.
22. [Operator #38] I want key material (signing keys, root credentials) backed up somewhere outside the primary infrastructure, so losing one system doesn't lock me out permanently.
23. [Operator #39] I want a clear answer to "what's my actual recovery time if X dies," verified in practice, not assumed from an architecture diagram.
24. [Operator #40] I want continuity plans sized to what a single person can actually execute alone under stress, not a playbook written for a team.
25. [Operator #41] I want to know my actual exposure to any single vendor's outage or pricing change, so a policy shift elsewhere doesn't blindside my whole platform.
26. [Operator #42] I want critical dependencies to have a documented fallback or exit path, so a vendor deprecating a service doesn't strand me with no migration plan.
27. [Operator #43] I want to track which vendors hold sensitive data or credentials, so a third-party breach's blast radius on me is knowable in advance, not discovered after the fact.
28. [Operator #44] I want pricing-tier changes from any dependency surfaced to me proactively, so a silent cost increase doesn't show up only on the invoice.
29. [Operator #45] I want to avoid hard lock-in to a single provider for anything I could plausibly need to migrate off of under pressure.
30. [Operator #46] I want vendor security incidents that affect my data to reach me directly (status page, security bulletin subscription), not discovered secondhand.
31. [Operator #47] I want contract/ToS changes from key vendors flagged, since I don't have legal counsel reviewing every update on my behalf.
32. [Operator #48] I want to periodically re-justify every recurring vendor cost against what it actually buys me, since subscriptions accumulate silently without a team enforcing a review cadence.
33. [Operator #49] I want an audit trail of who (which agent, which session) did what to production, since I'm the only person who can reconstruct an incident after the fact.
34. [Operator #50] I want data retention and deletion practices that are at least defensible if a user ever asks what I hold on them, even without a formal compliance program.
35. [Operator #51] I want a lightweight, honest privacy policy that matches what the platform actually does, not aspirational boilerplate I'd fail to uphold under scrutiny.
36. [Operator #52] I want to know exactly what user data flows through third-party AI providers, so I can represent that accurately if ever asked.
37. [Operator #53] I want logs of administrative actions (who changed a permission, who rotated a key) even when "who" is always me, so I have a timeline during an incident review.
38. [Operator #54] I want a minimal, honest terms-of-service and acceptable-use policy, so I have something to point to if a user's usage crosses a line.
39. [Operator #55] I want to avoid collecting data I don't have a clear use for, since unused data is pure downside risk with no offsetting benefit at my scale.
40. [Operator #56] I want an incident-disclosure plan drafted before an incident happens, since improvising a breach notice under pressure is where mistakes get made.

---

## 13. Customization, Configuration & Guardrail Automation

Making the defaults fit, and making sure automated policy substitutes for the review a solo operator doesn't have. 16 stories.

**User**
1. [User #57] I want to configure default behaviors (verbosity, model choice, permission mode) once, in a settings file, rather than restate them every session.
2. [User #58] I want per-project overrides to take precedence over global defaults, so one repo's conventions don't leak into another's.
3. [User #59] I want a documented, discoverable way to add my own skills/commands, so customization doesn't require reverse-engineering the system.
4. [User #60] I want permission rules I set to be inspectable, so I can audit what I've actually authorized over time.
5. [User #61] I want sensible defaults out of the box, so a new project isn't unusable until I've hand-tuned a dozen settings.
6. [User #62] I want to scope a capability to a specific directory or app, so a broad tool doesn't behave identically everywhere regardless of context.
7. [User #63] I want changes to my configuration to take effect without needing to fully restart or relearn the tool.
8. [User #64] I want to turn off any automated or proactive behavior I don't want, without losing the rest of the system's usefulness.

**Operator**
9. [Operator #57] I want policy-as-code guardrails (cost ceilings, permission scopes, deploy gates) enforced automatically, since there's no colleague to catch what automation misses.
10. [Operator #58] I want CI/CD to block a deploy that fails a security or cost check, rather than rely on me remembering to look every time.
11. [Operator #59] I want dependency vulnerabilities surfaced and triaged automatically, since I don't have a dedicated security team scanning for me.
12. [Operator #60] I want infrastructure changes to require a diff I can review quickly, so oversight scales with my limited time rather than requiring line-by-line manual audits.
13. [Operator #61] I want risky automation (auto-deploys, auto-scaling, auto-remediation) to default to notify-then-act with a delay, so I have a window to intervene before an automated fix makes things worse.
14. [Operator #62] I want a single command that shows overall platform health (cost, security posture, uptime) at a glance, since I don't have a team standup to surface it for me.
15. [Operator #63] I want guardrails to fail safe (deny by default) when their own logic is uncertain, since there's no second approver to catch a guardrail's own bug.
16. [Operator #64] I want the system to actively resist my own bad shortcuts under time pressure (skip the hook, force it through), since solo operation means the platform's discipline has to substitute for a team's.

---

## Story-Count Summary

| Area | Agent | Tool | Runtime | User | Operator | Total |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1. Discoverability, Naming & Documentation | 8 | 8 | 2 | – | – | 18 |
| 2. Contracts, Validation & Invocation | 9 | 8 | 1 | – | – | 18 |
| 3. Errors, Retries & Reliability | 11 | 8 | 1 | – | – | 20 |
| 4. State, Concurrency & Side Effects | 6 | 8 | 9 | – | – | 23 |
| 5. Context, Memory & Continuity | 8 | – | 8 | 8 | – | 24 |
| 6. Safety, Permissions & Trust Boundaries | 9 | 8 | 10 | 8 | – | 35 |
| 7. Cost, Budget & Resource Economics | 1 | – | 8 | 8 | 8 | 25 |
| 8. Observability, Audit & Feedback | 5 | 8 | 8 | – | 8 | 29 |
| 9. Composability, Orchestration & Ecosystem Fit | 6 | 8 | 8 | – | – | 22 |
| 10. Lifecycle, Versioning & Change Management | 1 | 8 | 9 | – | – | 18 |
| 11. Human Control, Communication & Collaboration | – | – | – | 32 | – | 32 |
| 12. Platform Security Operations | – | – | – | – | 40 | 40 |
| 13. Customization, Configuration & Guardrail Automation | – | – | – | 8 | 8 | 16 |
| **Total** | **64** | **64** | **64** | **64** | **64** | **320** |

Two things fall out of the shape of this table. **Safety/Permissions (35)** and **Platform Security Ops (40)** are the two heaviest areas by a wide margin — security is where the most perspectives collide and the platform-operator concerns are the most numerous single block. **Human Control/Communication (32)** is entirely one perspective with zero overlap — a reminder that the interactive-session experience is a distinct design surface from the infrastructure underneath it, not just "the agent talking more."
